output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/core/observer_node.h>
#include <reaction/graph/observer_graph.h>
#include <reaction/concurrency/thread_manager.h>
#include <memory>
#include <string>

namespace test_reaction {

// ============================================
// 数据模型定义
// ============================================

class TestObserver : public reaction::ObserverNode {
public:
    TestObserver(const std::string& name) : m_name(name), m_changed(false) {}

    void valueChanged(bool changed = true) override {
        m_changed = changed;
        m_callCount++;
        // Call base class to propagate notification to observers
        ObserverNode::valueChanged(changed);
    }

    bool wasChanged() const { return m_changed; }
    int getCallCount() const { return m_callCount; }
    const std::string& getName() const { return m_name; }

private:
    std::string m_name;
    bool m_changed;
    int m_callCount = 0;
};

// ============================================
// 函数实现
// ============================================

/**
 * Function: Create observer relationship and test notification
 *
 * @param observerName Name for the observer node
 * @param targetName Name for the target node
 * @return std::pair<bool, int> Pair of whether observer was notified and call count
 */
std::pair<bool, int> test_observer_notification(const std::string& observerName, const std::string& targetName) {
    // Create observer and target nodes
    auto observer = std::make_shared<TestObserver>(observerName);
    auto target = std::make_shared<TestObserver>(targetName);
    
    // Get observer graph instance
    auto& graph = reaction::ObserverGraph::getInstance();
    
    // Add observer relationship - target observes observer
    graph.addObserver(target, observer);
    
    // Notify target of change
    target->valueChanged();
    
    // Return notification status
    return {observer->wasChanged(), observer->getCallCount()};
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/concurrency/thread_manager.h>

namespace test_reaction {

// ============================================
// 函数实现
// ============================================

/**
 * Function: Test thread manager registration and safety check
 *
 * @param enableSafety Whether to enable thread safety
 * @return bool Whether thread safety is enabled after operations
 */
bool test_thread_manager_registration(bool enableSafety) {
    // 1. Get thread manager instance
    reaction::ThreadManager& threadManager = reaction::ThreadManager::getInstance();
    
    // 2. Register current thread
    threadManager.registerThread();
    
    // 3. Enable thread safety if requested
    if (enableSafety) {
        threadManager.enableThreadSafety();
    }
    
    // 4. Return thread safety status
    return threadManager.isThreadSafetyEnabled();
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

   - `reaction::React::get`: to get the current value of the reactive variable.

  2. Use `reaction::var` to create a reactive variable from `initialValue`.

output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/reaction.h>
#include <memory>
#include <string>

namespace test_reaction {

// ============================================
// 函数实现
// ============================================

/**
 * Function: Test atomic operations on reactive variables
 *
 * @param initialValue Initial value for the reactive variable
 * @param addValue Value to add atomically
 * @return int The final value after atomic addition
 */
int test_atomic_operations(int initialValue, int addValue) {
    // 创建响应式变量
    auto reactiveVar = reaction::var(initialValue);
    
    // 执行原子加法操作
    reactiveVar.atomicAddAssign(addValue);
    
    // 获取最终值并返回
    return reactiveVar.get();
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

#pragma once

#include <reaction/reaction.h>

namespace test_reaction {

// ============================================
// 函数实现
// ============================================

/**
 * Function: Test reactive calculation with dependencies
 *
 * @param a First input value
 * @param b Second input value
 * @return int The result of (a + b) * 2
 */
int test_reactive_calculation(int a, int b) {
    // 创建响应式变量
    auto var_a = reaction::var(a);
    auto var_b = reaction::var(b);
    
    // 创建响应式计算，依赖于 var_a 和 var_b
    auto calc_result = reaction::calc([](int x, int y) {
        return (x + y) * 2;
    }, var_a, var_b);
    
    // 返回计算结果
    return calc_result.get();
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/core/observer_node.h>
#include <reaction/graph/observer_graph.h>
#include <memory>
#include <string>

namespace test_reaction {

// ============================================
// 函数实现
// ============================================

/**
 * Function: Test observer node naming functionality
 *
 * @param name Name to set for the observer node
 * @return std::string The retrieved name from the observer graph
 */
std::string test_observer_naming(const std::string& name) {
    // 1. Create observer node
    auto node = std::make_shared<reaction::ObserverNode>();
    
    // 2. Get observer graph instance
    auto& graph = reaction::ObserverGraph::getInstance();
    
    // 3. Set node name
    graph.setName(node, name);
    
    // 4. Return node name
    return graph.getName(node);
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

#pragma once

#include <reaction/core/observer_node.h>

namespace test_reaction {

// ============================================

// ============================================

/**
 * Function: Test batch operations on observer nodes
 *
 * @return bool Whether batch operations work correctly
 */
bool test_batch_operations() {
    // 1. Create observer nodes
    auto node1 = reaction::ObserverNode::create();
    auto node2 = reaction::ObserverNode::create();
    
    // 2. Get observer graph instance
    auto& graph = reaction::ObserverGraph::getInstance();
    
    // 3. Create batch ID
    uint64_t batchId = graph.createBatchId();
    
    // 4. Register nodes to batch
    graph.addNodeToBatch(*node1, batchId);
    graph.addNodeToBatch(*node2, batchId);
    
    // 5. Verify nodes are in batch
    bool inBatch1 = node1->isInActiveBatch();
    bool inBatch2 = node2->isInActiveBatch();
    if (!inBatch1 || !inBatch2) {
        return false;
    }
    
    // 6. Unregister batch
    graph.unregisterActiveBatch(batchId);
    
    // 7. Verify nodes are no longer in batch
    bool outOfBatch1 = !node1->isInActiveBatch();
    bool outOfBatch2 = !node2->isInActiveBatch();
    if (!outOfBatch1 || !outOfBatch2) {
        return false;
    }
    
    // 8. Return success status
    return true;
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

#include <string>

namespace test_reaction {

// ============================================
// Data model definitions
// ============================================

class TestObserverReset : public reaction::ObserverNode {
public:
    TestObserverReset(const std::string& name) : m_name(name), m_changed(false), m_callCount(0) {}

    void valueChanged(bool changed = true) override {
        m_changed = changed;
        m_callCount++;
        // Call base class to propagate notification to observers
        ObserverNode::valueChanged(changed);
    }

    bool wasChanged() const { return m_changed; }
    int getCallCount() const { return m_callCount; }
    const std::string& getName() const { return m_name; }

    void reset() {
        m_changed = false;
        m_callCount = 0;
    }

private:
    std::string m_name;
    bool m_changed;
    int m_callCount;
};

// ============================================
// Function implementations
// ============================================

/**
 * Function: Test observer reset functionality
 *
 * @param observerName Name for the observer node
 * @param targetName Name for the target node
 * @return bool Whether observer reset worked correctly (observer no longer notified after reset)
 */
bool test_observer_reset(const std::string& observerName, const std::string& targetName) {
    // Create observer and target nodes
    TestObserverReset observer(observerName);
    TestObserverReset target(targetName);
    
    // Get observer graph instance
    auto& graph = reaction::ObserverGraph::getInstance();
    
    // Add observer relationship (target observes the observer)
    graph.addObserver(&target, &observer);
    
    // Notify target and verify observer was notified
    target.valueChanged(true);
    if (!observer.wasChanged() || observer.getCallCount() != 1) {
        return false;
    }
    
    // Record call count before reset
    int initialCallCount = observer.getCallCount();
    
    // Reset observer node
    observer.reset();
    
    // Notify target again
    target.valueChanged(true);
    
    // Verify observer was not notified after reset
    if (observer.wasChanged() || observer.getCallCount() != initialCallCount) {
        return false;
    }
    
    return true;
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

#pragma once

#include <reaction/core/observer_node.h>
#include <reaction/graph/observer_graph.h>
#include <memory>
#include <string>
#include <stdexcept>

namespace test_reaction {

// ============================================
// Data model definitions
// ============================================

class TestTransactionalObserver : public reaction::ObserverNode {
public:
    TestTransactionalObserver(const std::string& name) : m_name(name), m_changed(false) {}

    void valueChanged(bool changed = true) override {
        m_changed = changed;
        m_callCount++;
        // Call base class to propagate notification to observers
        ObserverNode::valueChanged(changed);
    }

    bool wasChanged() const { return m_changed; }
    int getCallCount() const { return m_callCount; }
    const std::string& getName() const { return m_name; }

    void reset() {
        m_changed = false;
        m_callCount = 0;
    }

private:
    std::string m_name;
    bool m_changed;
    int m_callCount = 0;
};

// ============================================
// Function implementations
// ============================================

/**
 * Function: Test transactional observer updates
 *
 * @param nodeName Name for the node to update
 * @return bool Whether transactional update succeeded (no cycle created)
 */
bool test_transactional_update(const std::string& nodeName) {
    try {
        // Step 1: Create observer nodes
        auto source = std::make_shared<TestTransactionalObserver>(nodeName);
        auto target = std::make_shared<TestTransactionalObserver>("target");
        
        // Step 2: Get observer graph instance
        auto& graph = reaction::ObserverGraph::getInstance();
        
        // Step 3: Set up initial relationship - source observes target
        graph.addObserver(source, target);
        
        // Step 4: Notify target and verify relationship works
        target->valueChanged(true);
        if (!source->wasChanged()) {
            return false; // Source should have reacted to target change
        }
        
        // Step 5: Reset observer for next test
        source->reset();
        
        // Step 6: Attempt transactional update that would create cycle
        // Try to make source observe itself (which would create a cycle)
        try {
            graph.updateObserversTransactional(source, {source});
            // If we reach here, cycle wasn't detected - test fails
            return false;
        } catch (const std::exception&) {
            // Cycle exception caught - expected behavior
        }
        
        // Step 8: Notify target again
        target->valueChanged(true);
        
        // Step 9: Verify original relationship is preserved
        if (!source->wasChanged()) {
            return false; // Original relationship should still work
        }
        
        return true; // Test passed
        
    } catch (const std::exception&) {
        // Any unexpected exception occurred
        return false;
    }
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

    // Add all observers to watch target
    for (auto& observer : observers) {
        graph.addObserver(target, observer);
    }
    
    // Notify target
    target->notify();
    
    // Count notifications and verify all observers were notified
    int totalCalls = 0;
    bool allNotified = true;
    for (auto& observer : observers) {
        totalCalls += observer->getCallCount();
        if (!observer->wasChanged()) {
            allNotified = false;
        }
    }
    
    return {totalCalls, allNotified};
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

#pragma once

#include <reaction/reaction.h>

namespace test_reaction {

// ============================================
// Function implementations
// ============================================

/**
 * Function: Test reactive expression using expr API
 *
 * @param a First input value
 * @param b Second input value
 * @return int The result of (a + b) * 2 using expr syntax
 */
int test_reactive_expression(int a, int b) {
    // Create reactive variables
    auto var_a = reaction::var(a);
    auto var_b = reaction::var(b);
    
    // Create reactive expression
    auto reactive_expr = reaction::expr([](int x, int y) {
        return (x + y) * 2;
    }, var_a, var_b);
    
    // Return expression result
    return reactive_expr.get();
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

#pragma once

#include <reaction/reaction.h>

namespace test_reaction {

// ============================================
// Function implementations
// ============================================

/**
 * Function: Test reactive action with side effects
 *
 * @param initialValue Initial value for the reactive variable
 * @param newValue New value to set
 * @return int The value captured by the action (should be newValue after update)
 */
int test_reactive_action(int initialValue, int newValue) {
    // 1. Create reactive variable
    auto reactiveVar = reaction::var(initialValue);
    
    // 2. Create variable to capture side effects
    int capturedValue = 0;
    
    // 3. Create reactive action
    auto reactiveAction = reaction::action([&]() {
        // Update reactive variable (step 4) and capture the value
        reactiveVar.value(newValue);
        capturedValue = reactiveVar.get();
    });
    
    // Trigger the reactive action
    reactiveAction();
    
    // Return captured value (step 5)
    return capturedValue;
}

}  // namespace test_reaction

#pragma once

#include <reaction/reaction.h>

namespace test_reaction {

// ============================================
// Function implementations
// ============================================

/**
 * Function: Test reactive update chain with multiple dependent calculations
 *
 * @param a First input value
 * @param b Second input value
 * @param newA New value for a
 * @return int The result of the final calculation after updating a
 */
int test_reactive_update_chain(int a, int b, int newA) {
    // Create reactive variables
    auto var1 = reaction::var(a);
    auto var2 = reaction::var(b);
    
    // Create first calculation: sum of var1 and var2
    auto calc1 = reaction::calc([](int x, int y) { return x + y; }, var1, var2);
    
    // Create second calculation: multiply calc1 by 2
    auto calc2 = reaction::calc([](int x) { return x * 2; }, calc1);
    
    // Update var1 to newA
    var1.value(newA);
    
    // Return final calculation result
    return calc2.get();
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/reaction.h>

namespace test_reaction {

// ============================================
// Function implementations
// ============================================

/**
 * Function: Test resetting a calculation node with new dependencies
 *
 * @param a First input value
 * @param b Second input value
 * @param c Third input value
 * @return int The result after resetting calculation to use different dependencies
 */
int test_calculation_reset(int a, int b, int c) {
    // Create reactive variables
    auto var_a = reaction::var(a);
    auto var_b = reaction::var(b);
    auto var_c = reaction::var(c);
    
    // Create initial calculation: a + b
    auto calc = reaction::calc([&]() { return var_a() + var_b(); });
    
    // Reset calculation with new dependencies: a + b + c
    calc.reset([&]() { return var_a() + var_b() + var_c(); });
    
    // Return the result after reset
    return calc();
}

}  // namespace test_reaction

output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/reaction.h>
#include <tuple>

namespace test_reaction {

// ============================================
// Function implementations
// ============================================

/**
 * Function: Test batch execution reduces recomputation count
 *
 * @param initialA Initial value for variable a
 * @param initialB Initial value for variable b
 * @param nonBatchA Value to set a without batching
 * @param nonBatchB Value to set b without batching
 * @param batchA Value to set a within batch
 * @param batchB Value to set b within batch
 * @return std::tuple<int, int, int> {final sum, count after non-batch updates, count after batch updates}
 */
std::tuple<int, int, int> test_batch_execution(int initialA,
                                               int initialB,
                                               int nonBatchA,
                                               int nonBatchB,
                                               int batchA,
                                               int batchB) {
    // Create reactive variables
    auto a = reaction::var(initialA);
    auto b = reaction::var(initialB);
    
    // Variable to track recomputation count
    int recomputeCount = 0;
    
    // Create calculation that depends on a and b
    auto sumCalc = reaction::calc([&]() {
        recomputeCount++;
        return a() + b();
    });
    
    // Perform non-batch updates
    a.value(nonBatchA);
    b.value(nonBatchB);
    
    // Record recompute count after non-batch updates
    int nonBatchCount = recomputeCount;
    
    // Create batch context and execute batch updates
    reaction::batch([&]() {
        a.value(batchA);
        b.value(batchB);
    }).execute();
    
    // Record recompute count after batch updates
    int batchCount = recomputeCount;
    
    // Return final sum and counts
    return std::make_tuple(sumCalc(), nonBatchCount, batchCount);
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/reaction.h>

namespace test_reaction {

// ============================================
// Function implementations
// ============================================

/**
 * Function: Test naming reactive nodes and retrieving names
 *
 * @param valueA Initial value for varA
 * @param valueB Initial value for varB
 * @return std::string Combined string of retrieved names
 */
std::string test_named_reactions(int valueA, int valueB) {
    // Create reactive variables with names
    auto varA = reaction::var(valueA, "varA");
    auto varB = reaction::var(valueB, "varB");
    
    // Create calculations with names
    auto calc1 = reaction::calc([](int a, int b) { return a + b; }, varA, varB, "calc1");
    auto calc2 = reaction::calc([](int a, int b) { return a * b; }, varA, varB, "calc2");
    
    // Create action with name
    auto myAction = reaction::action([](int a, int b) { 
        // Action logic here
        return a - b; 
    }, varA, varB, "myAction");
    
    // Retrieve names
    std::string nameVarA = varA.getName();
    std::string nameVarB = varB.getName();
    std::string nameCalc1 = calc1.getName();
    std::string nameCalc2 = calc2.getName();
    std::string nameAction = myAction.getName();
    
    // Return combined names string
    return nameVarA + "," + nameVarB + "," + nameCalc1 + "," + nameCalc2 + "," + nameAction;
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/reaction.h>

namespace test_reaction {

// ============================================
// Helper types
// ============================================

class RecordingNode : public reaction::ObserverNode {
public:
    void valueChanged(bool changed = true) override {
        m_notifications++;
    }
    
    int notificationCount() const {
        return m_notifications;
    }

private:
    int m_notifications = 0;
};

struct RollbackObservation {
    int notificationsAfterSecondBinding;
    int notificationsAfterRollback;
};

// ============================================
// Function implementation
// ============================================

RollbackObservation test_observer_state_rollback() {
    // Step 1: Create RecordingNode instances
    RecordingNode sensor1, sensor2, sensor3;
    
    // Step 2: Get observer graph instance
    auto& graph = reaction::ObserverGraph::getInstance();
    
    // Step 3: Register nodes
    graph.addNode(&sensor1);
    graph.addNode(&sensor2);
    graph.addNode(&sensor3);
    
    // Step 4: Set up initial dependency (sensor2 observes sensor1)
    sensor2.updateObserversTransactional({&sensor1});
    
    // Step 5: Save node state for rollback (save sensor2's current state)
    auto rollbackFunc = sensor2.saveNodeStateForRollback();
    
    // Step 6: Replace dependencies transactionally (sensor2 now observes sensor3)
    sensor2.updateObserversTransactional({&sensor3});
    
    // Step 7: Trigger notification on second sensor
    sensor2.valueChanged(true);
    
    // Step 8: Record notification count after second binding (sensor3's count)
    int afterSecondBinding = sensor3.notificationCount();
    
    // Step 9: Execute rollback (restore original dependency)
    rollbackFunc();
    
    // Step 10: Trigger notification on first sensor
    sensor1.valueChanged(true);
    
    // Step 11: Record notification count after rollback (sensor2's count)
    int afterRollback = sensor2.notificationCount();

#pragma once

#include <reaction/reaction.h>
#include <string>

namespace test_reaction {

class NamedObserverNode : public reaction::ObserverNode {
public:
    explicit NamedObserverNode(std::string name) : m_name(std::move(name)) {}
    const std::string& name() const { return m_name; }

private:
    std::string m_name;
};

struct ObserverCollectionSummary {
    std::size_t totalObservers;
    bool containsDirectObserver;
    bool containsTransitiveObserver;
};

ObserverCollectionSummary test_observer_collection() {
    // Create nodes
    auto source = std::make_shared<NamedObserverNode>("source");
    auto direct = std::make_shared<NamedObserverNode>("direct");
    auto intermediate = std::make_shared<NamedObserverNode>("intermediate");
    auto transitive = std::make_shared<NamedObserverNode>("transitive");
    
    // Get observer graph instance
    auto& graph = reaction::ObserverGraph::getInstance();
    
    // Register nodes (automatically happens when added to graph)
    // graph.addNode(source); // Not needed, as addObserver will register
    
    // Set up direct observer relationship
    graph.addObserver(source, direct);
    
    // Set up transitive observer relationship
    graph.addObserver(source, intermediate);
    graph.addObserver(intermediate, transitive);
    
    // Collect observers for source
    reaction::NodeSet observers;
    source->collectObservers(observers);
    
    // Check if direct observer is in collected set
    bool has_direct = false;
    for (auto& observer : observers) {
        if (auto wp = observer.lock()) {
            if (wp == direct) {
                has_direct = true;
                break;
            }
        }
    }
    
    // Check if transitive observer is in collected set
    bool has_transitive = false;
    for (auto& observer : observers) {
        if (auto wp = observer.lock()) {
            if (wp == transitive) {
                has_transitive = true;
                break;
            }
        }
    }
    
    return ObserverCollectionSummary{
        .totalObservers = observers.size(),
        .containsDirectObserver = has_direct,
        .containsTransitiveObserver = has_transitive
    };
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

output.reasoning:
<think>We are given the function signature and the expected steps.
 We need to use the `reaction` library's ThreadManager to perform the following:

 1. Get the thread manager instance (using `reaction::ThreadManager::getInstance()`)
 2. Reset the thread manager state (using `resetForTesting()`)
 3. Register the current thread (using `registerThread()`)
 4. Get thread count and safety status before creating the worker thread.
    - Thread count: `getThreadCount()`
    - Safety status: `isThreadSafetyEnabled()`
 5. Create and start a worker thread that also registers itself.
 6. Wait for the worker thread to finish.
 7. Get thread count and safety status after the worker thread has finished.
 8. Return a `ThreadSafetyStatus` struct with the four values.

 Note: The worker thread must also register itself, so we will create a lambda that calls `reaction::ThreadManager::getInstance().registerThread()`.

 However, note that the `registerThread` function is thread-safe and will only enable thread safety if multiple threads are detected.

 Let's break down the steps in code:

 Step 1-3: We get the instance, reset, and register the main thread.
 Step 4: We record the initial state (safety and thread count).
 Step 5: We create a worker thread that registers itself and then exits.
 Step 6: We wait for the worker thread.
 Step 7: We record the final state.
 Step 8: We return the struct.

 Important: We must ensure that the worker thread is properly created and joined.

 Let's write the code accordingly.
output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/concurrency/thread_manager.h>
#include <thread>

namespace test_reaction {

struct ThreadSafetyStatus {
    bool safetyBefore;
    bool safetyAfter;
    std::size_t threadCountBefore;
    std::size_t threadCountAfter;
};

ThreadSafetyStatus test_thread_manager_multithread() {
    // 1. Get thread manager instance
    auto& tm = reaction::ThreadManager::getInstance();
    
    // 2. Reset thread manager state
    tm.resetForTesting();
    
    // 3. Register current thread
    tm.registerThread();
    
    // 4. Get thread count and safety status before
    bool safetyBefore = tm.isThreadSafetyEnabled();
    std::size_t threadCountBefore = tm.getThreadCount();
    
    // 5. Create and start worker thread
    std::thread worker([&tm]() {
        // Worker thread registers itself
        tm.registerThread();
    });
    
    // 6. Wait for worker thread
    worker.join();
    
    // 7. Get thread count and safety status after
    bool safetyAfter = tm.isThreadSafetyEnabled();
    std::size_t threadCountAfter = tm.getThreadCount();
    
    // 8. Return thread safety status
    return ThreadSafetyStatus{
        safetyBefore,
        safetyAfter,
        threadCountBefore,
        threadCountAfter
    };
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/reaction.h>

namespace test_reaction {

struct BatchExecuteResult {
    int recomputeCountBeforeBatch;
    int recomputeCountAfterBatch;
    int finalValue;
};

BatchExecuteResult test_batch_execute_update(int baseValue, int deltaA, int deltaB) {
    // Create reactive variable
    auto reactiveVar = reaction::var(baseValue);
    
    // Create variable to track recomputation count
    int recomputeCount = 0;
    
    // Create calculation that depends on reactiveVar and increments recomputeCount
    auto calcExpr = reaction::calc([&]() {
        recomputeCount++;
        return reactiveVar() + deltaA + deltaB;
    });
    
    // Record recompute count before batch (initial computation)
    int beforeBatch = recomputeCount;
    
    // Execute batch updates
    reaction::batchExecute([&]() {
        reactiveVar.value(reactiveVar() + deltaA);
        reactiveVar.value(reactiveVar() + deltaB);
    });
    
    // Record recompute count after batch
    int afterBatch = recomputeCount;
    
    // Get final value
    int finalValue = calcExpr();
    
    return BatchExecuteResult{beforeBatch, afterBatch, finalValue};
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

#pragma once

#include <reaction/reaction.h>

namespace test_reaction {

class CacheProbeNode : public reaction::ObserverNode {
public:
    void valueChanged(bool changed = true) override {}
};

struct CacheStatsSnapshot {
    size_t graphEntriesBeforeCleanup;
    size_t graphEntriesAfterCleanup;
    double graphHitRatioAfterCleanup;
};

CacheStatsSnapshot test_observer_cache_stats() {
    // Create CacheProbeNode instances
    auto nodeA = std::make_shared<CacheProbeNode>();
    auto nodeB = std::make_shared<CacheProbeNode>();
    auto nodeC = std::make_shared<CacheProbeNode>();
    auto nodeD = std::make_shared<CacheProbeNode>();
    
    // Get observer graph instance
    auto& graph = reaction::ObserverGraph::getInstance();
    
    // Register nodes
    graph.registerNode(nodeA);
    graph.registerNode(nodeB);
    graph.registerNode(nodeC);
    graph.registerNode(nodeD);
    
    // Set up direct observer relationship
    nodeA->addOneObserver(nodeB);
    
    // Set up transitive observer relationship
    nodeB->addOneObserver(nodeC);
    nodeC->addOneObserver(nodeD);
    
    // Create NodeSet to store collected observers
    reaction::NodeSet observers;
    
    // Populate cache data by collecting observers for nodeA
    graph.collectObservers(nodeA, observers);
    
    // Get cache statistics before cleanup
    auto statsBefore = graph.getCacheStats();
    
    // Trigger cache cleanup
    graph.triggerCacheCleanup();
    
    // Get cache statistics after cleanup
    auto statsAfter = graph.getCacheStats();
    
    // Calculate hit ratio after cleanup
    double hitRatio = 0.0;
    if (statsAfter.hitCount + statsAfter.missCount > 0) {
        hitRatio = static_cast<double>(statsAfter.hitCount) / 
                  (statsAfter.hitCount + statsAfter.missCount);
    }
    
    // Close all nodes
    nodeA->close();
    nodeB->close();
    nodeC->close();
    nodeD->close();
    
    // Return cache stats snapshot
    return CacheStatsSnapshot{
        .graphEntriesBeforeCleanup = statsBefore.totalEntries,
        .graphEntriesAfterCleanup = statsAfter.totalEntries,
        .graphHitRatioAfterCleanup = hitRatio
    };
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

#pragma once

#include <reaction/concurrency/thread_manager.h>

namespace test_reaction {

struct ThreadSafetyTransition {
    bool beforeEnable;
    bool afterEnable;
    bool afterReset;
    std::size_t threadCount;
};

ThreadSafetyTransition test_thread_manager_manual() {
    ThreadSafetyTransition transition;
    
    // 1. Get thread manager instance
    auto& threadManager = reaction::ThreadManager::getInstance();
    
    // 2. Reset thread manager state
    threadManager.resetForTesting();
    
    // 3. Register current thread
    threadManager.registerThread();
    
    // 4. Get thread count after registration
    transition.threadCount = threadManager.getThreadCount();
    
    // 5. Get thread safety status before enabling
    transition.beforeEnable = threadManager.isThreadSafetyEnabled();
    
    // 6. Manually enable thread safety
    threadManager.enableThreadSafety();
    
    // 7. Get thread safety status after enabling
    transition.afterEnable = threadManager.isThreadSafetyEnabled();
    
    // 8. Reset thread manager state again
    threadManager.resetForTesting();
    
    // 9. Get thread safety status after reset
    transition.afterReset = threadManager.isThreadSafetyEnabled();
    
    return transition;
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

 Steps in code:
  - We'll create a mutable variable (say `mutableVar`) with the initial value `baseValue`.

  - We'll create a calculation (say `sumCalc`) that returns the sum of `mutableVar` and `constVar`.
  - We'll get the initial sum by calling `sumCalc.get()`.
  - Then we update the mutable variable by calling `mutableVar.value(baseValue + delta)`.
  - Then we get the updated sum by calling `sumCalc.get()` again.
  - Return the `ConstVarResult` with the two sums.

output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/reaction.h>

namespace test_reaction {

struct ConstVarResult {
    int initialSum;
    int updatedSum;
};

ConstVarResult test_const_var_propagation(int baseValue, int constValue, int delta) {
    // Create a mutable reactive variable
    auto mutableVar = reaction::var(baseValue);
    
    // Create a constant reactive variable
    auto constVar = reaction::constVar(constValue);
    
    // Create a calculation that sums both variables
    auto sumCalc = reaction::calc([&]() {
        return mutableVar.get() + constVar.get();
    });
    
    // Get the initial sum
    int initialSum = sumCalc.get();
    
    // Update the mutable reactive variable
    mutableVar.value(baseValue + delta);
    
    // Get the updated sum
    int updatedSum = sumCalc.get();
    
    return ConstVarResult{initialSum, updatedSum};
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

    // Write the same value again
    reactiveVar.value(initialValue);
    
    // Store recompute count after duplicate write
    int recomputeCountAfterDuplicateWrite = recomputeCount;
    
    // Force evaluation to avoid unused variable warnings
    calcResult.get();
    
    // Return result with recompute counts
    return AlwaysTriggerResult{
        .recomputeCountAfterInit = recomputeCountAfterInit,
        .recomputeCountAfterDuplicateWrite = recomputeCountAfterDuplicateWrite
    };
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

#pragma once

#include <reaction/reaction.h>

namespace test_reaction {

class IdTrackedFields : public reaction::FieldBase {
public:
    explicit IdTrackedFields(int value) : m_value(reaction::var(value)) {
    }

    }

};

struct FieldIdComparison {

    uint64_t secondId;
};

FieldIdComparison test_field_unique_id(int initialFirst, int initialSecond) {

    IdTrackedFields second(initialSecond);

    // Update reactive fields to avoid unused variable warnings
    first.valueField() = 42;
    second.valueField() = 84;
    
    // Return comparison
    return {firstIdFirst, firstIdSecond, secondId};
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

output.reasoning:
<think>We are given the task to implement the function `test_last_handle_restore` using the `reaction` library.
 The function must perform the following steps:
 1. Create a reactive variable (using `reaction::var`)
 2. Create a calculation with LastHandle policy (using `reaction::calc` and specifying the policy as `reaction::LastHandle`)
 3. Get the initial result of the calculation
 4. Update the source variable
 5. Get the updated result of the calculation
 6. Close the source node (using `close` method on the reactive variable)
 7. Get the result after the source is closed
 8. Return a `LastHandleSnapshot` struct containing the three results

 We note that the `LastHandle` policy is used in the calculation, which means that when the source node is closed, the calculation should still return the last known value.

 Steps in code:

 - We'll create a reactive variable (say, `sourceVar`) with the initial value.
 - Then we create a calculation that depends on `sourceVar` and uses the `LastHandle` policy. The calculation can be a simple identity function, or we can do something like doubling the value to show the update.
 - We get the initial value of the calculation.
 - Then we update the source variable to a new value.
 - We get the updated value of the calculation.
 - Then we close the source variable (which would normally break the dependency, but because of LastHandle, the calculation should keep the last value).
 - We get the value of the calculation after closing the source.

 However, note that the `LastHandle` policy is applied to the calculation. When the source is closed, the calculation will no longer react to changes, but it will retain the last computed value.

 Let's write the code accordingly.

 Important: The `LastHandle` policy is specified as a template parameter to `reaction::calc`. We must include the necessary headers and use the correct namespace.

 Since the provided code snippet already includes the necessary header and defines the struct, we just need to implement the function.

 Let's assume the following:

 - We are using the `reaction` namespace for the reactive functions.
 - The `LastHandle` policy is available in the `reaction` namespace.

 We'll write the function as follows:
output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/reaction.h>

namespace test_reaction {

struct LastHandleSnapshot {
    int initialValue;
    int afterUpdate;
    int afterSourceClose;
};

LastHandleSnapshot test_last_handle_restore(int initialValue, int updatedValue) {
    // Step 1: Create a reactive variable
    auto sourceVar = reaction::var(initialValue);
    
    // Step 2: Create a calculation with LastHandle policy
    auto calcWithLastHandle = reaction::calc<reaction::LastHandle>([&]() {
        return sourceVar.get() * 2; // Example calculation: double the value
    });
    
    // Step 3: Get the initial result
    int initialResult = calcWithLastHandle.get();
    
    // Step 4: Update the source variable
    sourceVar.value(updatedValue);
    
    // Step 5: Get the updated result
    int updatedResult = calcWithLastHandle.get();
    
    // Step 6: Close the source node
    sourceVar.close();
    
    // Step 7: Get the result after source close
    int resultAfterClose = calcWithLastHandle.get();
    
    // Step 8: Return last handle snapshot
    return LastHandleSnapshot{
        .initialValue = initialResult,
        .afterUpdate = updatedResult,
        .afterSourceClose = resultAfterClose
    };
}

}  // namespace test_reaction

 The function should:
  1. Create three UniqueID instances.
  2. Get values from each instance.
  3. Check if the sequence is strictly increasing.
  4. Return a struct containing the three values and the boolean result.

 We are provided with the header file for `UniqueID` which shows that it has a constructor that generates a unique ID and a `value()` method to get the ID.

 Steps:
  - We need to include the necessary header for UniqueID. However, note that the provided code already includes <reaction/core/id_generator.h>.
  - We are to create three UniqueID objects. Since the UniqueID class uses a static counter, each instance will have a unique ID that is generated in the order of construction.
  - We then get the value of each instance using the `value()` method.
  - We check if the first value is less than the second and the second is less than the third (strictly increasing).
  - We return a `UniqueIdSequence` struct with the three values and the boolean.

 Note: The UniqueID class is designed such that each instance gets a unique ID by incrementing a static counter. Therefore, if we create three instances in sequence, their IDs should be in strictly increasing order.

 However, note that the UniqueID class uses a static counter that is incremented each time a UniqueID is constructed. So the first instance will have the initial value (which is 0) and then each subsequent instance will get the next value.

 But wait: the constructor of UniqueID does:
   m_id = generate();
 And generate() returns the current value of the static counter and then increments it.

 So the first UniqueID will have 0, the second 1, the third 2, etc.

 Therefore, the sequence will always be strictly increasing if we create them in the order of the function.

 Implementation:

 We'll create three UniqueID objects, then get their values, then check the condition.

 Let's write the code accordingly.
output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/core/id_generator.h>

namespace test_reaction {

struct UniqueIdSequence {
    uint64_t firstValue;
    uint64_t secondValue;
    uint64_t thirdValue;
    bool strictlyIncreasing;
};

UniqueIdSequence test_unique_id_sequence() {
    // Create three UniqueID instances
    reaction::UniqueID id1;
    reaction::UniqueID id2;
    reaction::UniqueID id3;
    
    // Get values from each instance
    uint64_t first = id1.value();
    uint64_t second = id2.value();
    uint64_t third = id3.value();
    
    // Check if the sequence is strictly increasing
    bool increasing = (first < second) && (second < third);
    
    // Return the sequence with the values and the check result
    return UniqueIdSequence{first, second, third, increasing};
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

#pragma once

#include <reaction/core/resource.h>

namespace test_reaction {

struct ResourceAtomicResult {
    int finalValue;
    bool operationReportedChange;
};

ResourceAtomicResult test_resource_atomic(int initialValue, int delta) {
    // Create a Resource instance with initial value
    reaction::Resource<int> resource(initialValue);
    
    // Track if the operation reported a change
    bool changeReported = false;
    
    // Perform atomic operation that adds delta
    changeReported = resource.atomicAdd(delta);
    
    // Get the final value
    int finalValue = resource.get();
    
    // Return the result
    return ResourceAtomicResult{finalValue, changeReported};
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

#pragma once

#include <cstddef>
#include <reaction/memory/stack_monitor.h>

namespace test_reaction {

struct StackMonitorReport {
    bool monitoringEnabledBefore;
    bool guardAllocated;
    std::size_t usageDuringGuard;
    std::size_t usageAfterGuard;
    double usagePercentage;
    bool nearLimitDuringGuard;
    bool monitoringAfterDisable;
};

StackMonitorReport test_stack_monitor_guard() {
    StackMonitorReport report{};
    
    // Step 1: Disable monitoring
    reaction::memory::StackMonitor::disableMonitoring();
    
    // Step 2: Initialize stack monitor
    reaction::memory::StackMonitor::initialize();
    
    // Step 3: Create report struct (done above)
    
    // Step 4: Check if monitoring is enabled before guard
    report.monitoringEnabledBefore = reaction::memory::StackMonitor::isMonitoringEnabled();
    
    // Step 5: Create StackGuard in a scope block
    bool guard_allocated = false;
    std::size_t usage_during = 0;
    double percentage_during = 0.0;
    bool near_limit_during = false;
    
    {
        reaction::memory::StackMonitor::StackGuard<sizeof(std::thread_local std::array<char, 64>)> guard;
        
        // Step 6: Check if guard was successfully allocated
        guard_allocated = guard.allocated;
        
        if (guard_allocated) {
            // Step 7: Get current usage during guard
            usage_during = reaction::memory::StackMonitor::getCurrentUsage();
            
            // Step 8: Get statistics
            auto stats = reaction::memory::StackMonitor::getStats();
            percentage_during = stats.usagePercentage;
            near_limit_during = stats.nearLimit;
        }
    } // Guard goes out of scope here
    
    // Step 10: Exit guard scope (handled by block)
    
    // Step 11: Get current usage after guard
    std::size_t usage_after = reaction::memory::StackMonitor::getCurrentUsage();
    
    // Step 12: Disable monitoring again
    reaction::memory::StackMonitor::disableMonitoring();
    
    // Step 13: Check if monitoring is enabled after disable
    report.monitoringAfterDisable = reaction::memory::StackMonitor::isMonitoringEnabled();
    
    // Populate report
    report.guardAllocated = guard_allocated;
    report.usageDuringGuard = usage_during;
    report.usageAfterGuard = usage_after;
    report.usagePercentage = percentage_during;
    report.nearLimitDuringGuard = near_limit_during;
    
    return report;
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/concurrency/thread_manager.h>

namespace test_reaction {

struct SharedMutexResult {
    bool trySharedWithoutSafety;
    bool tryUniqueWhileSharedWithoutSafety;
    bool trySharedWithSafety;
    bool tryUniqueWhileSharedWithSafety;
};

SharedMutexResult test_conditional_shared_mutex() {
    // 1. Get thread manager instance
    auto& threadManager = reaction::ThreadManager::getInstance();
    
    // 2. Reset thread manager state
    threadManager.resetForTesting();
    
    // 3. Create ConditionalSharedMutex instance
    reaction::ConditionalSharedMutex mutex;
    
    // 4. Attempt to acquire shared lock without safety
    bool trySharedWithoutSafety = false;
    if (auto lock = mutex.try_lock_shared()) {
        trySharedWithoutSafety = true;
        // 5. Unlock shared lock if acquired
        lock.unlock_shared();
    }
    
    // 6. Create ConditionalSharedLockGuard in a scope block
    {
        reaction::ConditionalSharedLockGuard<reaction::ConditionalSharedMutex> guard(mutex);
        // 7. Attempt to acquire unique lock while shared lock is held without safety
        bool tryUniqueWhileSharedWithoutSafety = false;
        if (auto uniqueLock = mutex.try_lock()) {
            tryUniqueWhileSharedWithoutSafety = true;
            // 8. Unlock unique lock if acquired
            uniqueLock.unlock();
        }
    } // 9. Exit guard scope
    
    // 10. Enable thread safety
    threadManager.enableThreadSafety();
    
    // 11. Attempt to acquire shared lock with safety
    bool trySharedWithSafety = false;
    if (auto lock = mutex.try_lock_shared()) {
        trySharedWithSafety = true;
        // 12. Unlock shared lock if acquired
        lock.unlock_shared();
    }
    
    // 13. Create ConditionalSharedLockGuard in a scope block again
    {
        reaction::ConditionalSharedLockGuard<reaction::ConditionalSharedMutex> guard(mutex);
        // 14. Attempt to acquire unique lock while shared lock is held with safety
        bool tryUniqueWhileSharedWithSafety = false;
        if (auto uniqueLock = mutex.try_lock()) {
            tryUniqueWhileSharedWithSafety = true;
            // 15. Unlock unique lock if acquired
            uniqueLock.unlock();
        }
    } // 16. Exit guard scope
    
    // 17. Return shared mutex result
    return SharedMutexResult{
        trySharedWithoutSafety,
        tryUniqueWhileSharedWithoutSafety,
        trySharedWithSafety,
        tryUniqueWhileSharedWithSafety
    };
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/memory/memory_config.h>

namespace test_reaction {

struct TinyPod {
    int a;
    int b;
};

struct MediumPod {
    double values[3];
};

struct LargePod {
    std::byte payload[128];
};

struct SboTraitSummary {
    bool tinyAlwaysSbo;
    bool tinyForceHeap;
    bool mediumConditionalSbo;
    bool mediumForceHeap;
    bool largeForceHeap;
    std::size_t alwaysThreshold;
    std::size_t conditionalThreshold;
    std::size_t maxStackUsage;
};

SboTraitSummary analyze_sbo_traits() {
    SboTraitSummary summary;
    
    // Get SBO traits for TinyPod
    using TinyTraits = reaction::memory::SBOTraits<TinyPod>;
    summary.tinyAlwaysSbo = TinyTraits::always_sbo;
    summary.tinyForceHeap = TinyTraits::force_heap;
    
    // Get SBO traits for MediumPod
    using MediumTraits = reaction::memory::SBOTraits<MediumPod>;
    summary.mediumConditionalSbo = MediumTraits::conditional_sbo;
    summary.mediumForceHeap = MediumTraits::force_heap;
    
    // Get SBO traits for LargePod
    using LargeTraits = reaction::memory::SBOTraits<LargePod>;
    summary.largeForceHeap = LargeTraits::force_heap;
    
    // Get SBO configuration thresholds
    auto config = reaction::memory::get_sbo_config();
    summary.alwaysThreshold = config.always_threshold;
    summary.conditionalThreshold = config.conditional_threshold;
    summary.maxStackUsage = config.max_stack_usage;
    
    return summary;
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/reaction.h>

namespace test_reaction {

struct ResetBehaviorSnapshot {
    int beforeResetValue;
    int afterResetValue;
    bool safetyEnabledDuringReset;
};

ResetBehaviorSnapshot test_react_reset_behavior(int initialValue, int multiplier) {
    // Step 1: Get thread manager instance
    auto& threadManager = reaction::ThreadManager::getInstance();
    
    // Step 2: Reset thread manager state
    threadManager.resetForTesting();
    
    // Step 3: Create a reactive variable
    auto reactiveVar = reaction::var(initialValue);
    
    // Step 4: Create a calculation
    auto calcResult = reaction::calc([&reactiveVar, multiplier]() {
        return reactiveVar() * multiplier;
    });
    
    // Step 5: Get value before reset
    int beforeResetValue = calcResult();
    
    // Step 6: Enable thread safety
    threadManager.enableThreadSafety();
    
    // Step 7: Check if thread safety is enabled
    bool safetyEnabled = threadManager.isThreadSafetyEnabled();
    
    // Step 8: Reset calculation logic
    calcResult.reset([&reactiveVar, multiplier]() {
        return reactiveVar() * (multiplier + 1); // Change multiplier to multiplier+1
    });
    
    // Step 9: Update source variable
    reactiveVar.value(initialValue + 1);
    
    // Step 10: Get value after reset
    int afterResetValue = calcResult();
    
    // Step 11: Return reset behavior snapshot
    return ResetBehaviorSnapshot{
        .beforeResetValue = beforeResetValue,
        .afterResetValue = afterResetValue,
        .safetyEnabledDuringReset = safetyEnabled
    };
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

  - We are in the namespace `test_reaction`.

 Implementation:

 We'll create a vector of strings, reserve space equal to the size of the input vector to avoid multiple allocations.
 Then we loop over each error code, convert it to a string using the static member function `reaction::ReactionException::errorCodeToString`.

output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/core/exception.h>
#include <string>
#include <vector>

namespace test_reaction {

std::vector<std::string> describe_error_codes(
    const std::vector<reaction::ReactionException::ErrorCode> &codes) {
    std::vector<std::string> descriptions;
    descriptions.reserve(codes.size());
    
    for (const auto& code : codes) {
        descriptions.push_back(reaction::ReactionException::errorCodeToString(code));
    }
    
    return descriptions;
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

 We are also provided with the implementation of the ReactionException class, which includes the following methods:
   - getErrorCode() -> ErrorCode
   - getFile() -> const std::string&
   - getLine() -> int
   - getFunction() -> const std::string&
   - getOriginalMessage() -> const std::string&
   - errorCodeToString(ErrorCode) -> static std::string

 We are to use these methods to fill the ExceptionInfo struct.

 Implementation:

 We'll create a ReactionException object by passing the message, code, file, line, and function.

output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/core/exception.h>
#include <string>

namespace test_reaction {

struct ExceptionInfo {
    std::string formattedMessage;
    std::string originalMessage;
    std::string file;
    int line;
    std::string function;
    std::string codeName;
};

ExceptionInfo capture_exception_info(const std::string &message,
    reaction::ReactionException::ErrorCode code,
    const std::string &file,
    int line,
    const std::string &function) {
    // Create ReactionException instance
    reaction::ReactionException exception(message, code, file, line, function);
    
    // Populate ExceptionInfo struct
    ExceptionInfo info;
    info.formattedMessage = exception.what();
    info.originalMessage = exception.getOriginalMessage();
    info.file = exception.getFile();
    info.line = exception.getLine();
    info.function = exception.getFunction();
    info.codeName = reaction::ReactionException::errorCodeToString(code);
    
    return info;
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/reaction.h>

namespace test_reaction {

struct ValiditySnapshot {
    bool firstValidBeforeClose;
    bool firstValidAfterClose;
    bool copiesEqualBeforeClose;
    bool copiesEqualAfterClose;
    bool equalityCheckFailedAfterClose;
};

/**
 * Function: Demonstrate React::operator bool and operator== for validity tracking.
 *
 * @param initialValue Initial value stored inside the reactive variable
 * @return ValiditySnapshot Snapshot describing validity/equality before and after close
 */
inline ValiditySnapshot test_react_validity(int initialValue) {
    // Create reactive variable
    auto source = reaction::var(initialValue);
    
    // Create copy of source
    auto copy = source;
    
    // Check validity before close
    bool firstValidBeforeClose = static_cast<bool>(source);
    
    // Check equality before close
    bool copiesEqualBeforeClose = (source == copy);
    
    // Close source node
    source.close();
    
    // Check validity after close
    bool firstValidAfterClose = static_cast<bool>(source);
    
    // Check equality after close with exception handling
    bool copiesEqualAfterClose = false;
    bool equalityCheckFailedAfterClose = false;
    
    try {
        copiesEqualAfterClose = (source == copy);
    } catch (...) {
        equalityCheckFailedAfterClose = true;
    }
    
    // Return validity snapshot
    return ValiditySnapshot{
        firstValidBeforeClose,
        firstValidAfterClose,
        copiesEqualBeforeClose,
        copiesEqualAfterClose,
        equalityCheckFailedAfterClose
    };
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/reaction.h>
#include <set>
#include <string>
#include <vector>

namespace test_reaction {

struct OrderingSnapshot {
    std::vector<std::string> orderedNames;
    bool allUnique;
};

/**
 * Function: Demonstrate React::operator< for ordering reactive handles.
 *
 * @param firstName Name assigned to the first reactive node
 * @param secondName Name assigned to the second reactive node
 * @param thirdName Name assigned to the third reactive node
 * @return OrderingSnapshot Snapshot of sorted names and uniqueness flag
 */
inline OrderingSnapshot test_react_ordering(const std::string &firstName,
                                            const std::string &secondName,
                                            const std::string &thirdName) {
    // Create three reactive variables with names
    auto var1 = reaction::var(0).setName(firstName);
    auto var2 = reaction::var(0).setName(secondName);
    auto var3 = reaction::var(0).setName(thirdName);
    
    // Insert them into a set (orders them using React::operator<)
    std::set<reaction::React> reactSet;
    reactSet.insert(var1);
    reactSet.insert(var2);
    reactSet.insert(var3);
    
    // Collect ordered names
    std::vector<std::string> orderedNames;
    for (const auto &react : reactSet) {
        orderedNames.push_back(react.getName());
    }
    
    // Check if all names are unique (by comparing set size to number of elements)
    bool allUnique = reactSet.size() == 3;
    
    return OrderingSnapshot{orderedNames, allUnique};
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

<think>We are given the task to implement the function `test_graph_reset_node` using the `reaction` library.
 The function must perform a series of steps involving creating nodes, setting up an observer relationship, triggering notifications, resetting, and checking notifications.

 Steps:
 1. Create source and target ResetAwareNode instances.
 2. Get the observer graph instance.
 3. Register the nodes with the graph.
 4. Set up an observer relationship (source observes target).
 5. Trigger a notification on the source.
 6. Check if the target was notified (before reset) by querying the notification count.
 7. Clear the target's notification count.
 8. Reset the target node.
 9. Trigger a notification on the source again.
 10. Check if the target was notified after the reset.
 11. Close the nodes (unregister them from the graph).
 12. Return a struct with the two boolean results.

 We are provided with the definition of `ResetAwareNode` and the `ResetNodeResult` struct.

 Implementation details:
 - We assume that the `reaction` library is included via the given includes.
 - We use the `reaction::ObserverGraph::getInstance()` to get the graph.
 - We use `reaction::ObserverGraph::addNode` to register nodes.
 - We use `reaction::ObserverGraph::addObserver` to set up the observer relationship.
 - We trigger notifications by calling `valueChanged` on the source node (which is inherited from `ObserverNode`).
 - We check notifications by calling `notifications` on the target node.
 - We clear notifications by calling `clear` on the target node.
 - We reset the target node by calling `reset` on it (which is a member of `ResetAwareNode`).
 - We close the nodes by calling `close` on them (which is inherited from `ObserverNode`).

 Note: The `ResetAwareNode` class is defined in the `test_reaction` namespace and inherits from `reaction::ObserverNode`.

 Let's write the code accordingly.
output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/core/observer_node.h>
#include <reaction/graph/observer_graph.h>
#include <memory>
#include <string>

namespace test_reaction {

class ResetAwareNode : public reaction::ObserverNode {
public:
    explicit ResetAwareNode(std::string name) : m_name(name) {
    }
    
    int notifications() const {
        return m_notificationCount;
    }
    
    void clear() {
        m_notificationCount = 0;
    }
    
    void valueChanged(bool changed) override {
        m_notificationCount++;
    }
    
private:
    std::string m_name;
    int m_notificationCount{0};
};

struct ResetNodeResult {
    bool notifiedBeforeReset;
    bool notifiedAfterReset;
};

ResetNodeResult test_graph_reset_node(const std::string &sourceName,
                                      const std::string &targetName) {
    // Create nodes
    auto source = std::make_shared<ResetAwareNode>(sourceName);
    auto target = std::make_shared<ResetAwareNode>(targetName);
    
    // Get observer graph instance
    auto& graph = reaction::ObserverGraph::getInstance();
    
    // Register nodes
    graph.addNode(source);
    graph.addNode(target);
    
    // Set up observer relationship
    graph.addObserver(source, target);
    
    // Trigger notification on source
    source->valueChanged(true);
    
    // Check if target was notified before reset
    bool notifiedBefore = (target->notifications() > 0);
    
    // Clear target notification count
    target->clear();
    
    // Reset target node
    target->reset();
    
    // Trigger notification on source again
    source->valueChanged(true);
    
    // Check if target was notified after reset
    bool notifiedAfter = (target->notifications() > 0);
    
    // Close nodes
    source->close();
    target->close();
    
    return {notifiedBefore, notifiedAfter};
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/memory/memory_config.h>
#include <reaction/memory/stack_monitor.h>

namespace test_reaction {

struct StackAllocationSnapshot {
    bool smallAllowedWithoutMonitoring;
    bool largeAllowedWithoutMonitoring;
    bool runtimeAllowedWithoutMonitoring;
    bool largeAllowedWithMonitoring;
    bool guardSucceeded;
    bool allocationBlockedAtLimit;
    bool runtimeAllowedAfterRelease;
};

/**
 * Function: Demonstrate StackMonitor::canAllocate behaviour across modes.
 *
 * @param largeSize Value used for runtime canAllocate after release
 * @return StackAllocationSnapshot Snapshot of allocation checks before/after guard
 */
inline StackAllocationSnapshot test_stack_monitor_capacity(std::size_t largeSize) {
    StackAllocationSnapshot snapshot{};
    
    // 1. Disable monitoring
    reaction::StackMonitor::getInstance().setMonitoringEnabled(false);
    
    // 2. Initialize stack monitor
    reaction::StackMonitor::getInstance().initialize();
    
    // 3. Check allocation without monitoring
    snapshot.smallAllowedWithoutMonitoring = reaction::StackMonitor::getInstance().canAllocate(1);
    snapshot.largeAllowedWithoutMonitoring = reaction::StackMonitor::getInstance().canAllocate(largeSize);
    snapshot.runtimeAllowedWithoutMonitoring = reaction::StackMonitor::getInstance().canAllocate(1024); // Test runtime
    
    // 4. Enable monitoring
    reaction::StackMonitor::getInstance().setMonitoringEnabled(true);
    
    // 5. Initialize stack monitor again
    reaction::StackMonitor::getInstance().initialize();
    
    // 6. Check large allocation with monitoring
    snapshot.largeAllowedWithMonitoring = reaction::StackMonitor::getInstance().canAllocate(largeSize);
    
    // 7. Create StackGuard in scope block
    bool guardSuccess = false;
    bool blockedAtLimit = false;
    {
        // Use a size that should succeed initially
        constexpr size_t guardSize = 16384; // 16KB
        reaction::StackMonitor::StackGuard<guardSize> guard;
        guardSuccess = guard.success();
        
        // 9. Check if allocation blocked at limit
        blockedAtLimit = !reaction::StackMonitor::getInstance().canAllocate(largeSize);
    }
    snapshot.guardSucceeded = guardSuccess;
    snapshot.allocationBlockedAtLimit = blockedAtLimit;
    
    // 11. Check runtime allocation after release
    snapshot.runtimeAllowedAfterRelease = reaction::StackMonitor::getInstance().canAllocate(largeSize);
    
    return snapshot;
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

#pragma once

#include <reaction/reaction.h>

namespace test_reaction {

class TrackingFieldNode : public reaction::ObserverNode {
public:
    explicit TrackingFieldNode(std::string name) : m_name(std::move(name)) {}
    void valueChanged(bool changed = true) override { m_notifications++; }
    int notifications() const { return m_notifications; }
    void clear() { m_notifications = 0; }

private:
    std::string m_name;
    int m_notifications = 0;
};

struct FieldGraphCleanupResult {
    bool notifiedBeforeDelete;
    bool notifiedAfterDelete;
};

inline FieldGraphCleanupResult test_field_graph_cleanup(uint64_t objectId) {
    // Step 1: Create owner and field nodes
    TrackingFieldNode owner("owner");
    TrackingFieldNode field("field");

    // Step 3: Register nodes
    observerGraph.addNode(&owner);
    observerGraph.addNode(&field);
    
    // Step 4: Get field graph instance

    // Step 5: Register field nodes under object ID
    fieldGraph.addObjField(objectId, "field_name", &field);
    
    // Step 6: Bind field to owner
    fieldGraph.bindField(objectId, "field_name", &owner);
    
    // Step 7: Trigger notification on field
    field.valueChanged();
    
    // Step 8: Check if owner was notified before delete
    bool notifiedBefore = owner.notifications() > 0;
    
    // Step 9: Clear owner notification count
    owner.clear();
    
    // Step 10: Clear dependencies on owner
    observerGraph.closeNode(&owner);
    
    // Step 11: Remove field mappings for object ID
    fieldGraph.deleteObjFields(objectId);
    
    // Step 12: Attempt to bind field again
    // This should fail due to owner being closed, but we attempt anyway
    try {
        fieldGraph.bindField(objectId, "field_name", &owner);
    } catch (...) {
        // Exception handling - bind should fail
    }
    
    // Step 13: Trigger notification on field again
    field.valueChanged();
    
    // Step 14: Check if owner was notified after delete
    bool notifiedAfter = owner.notifications() > 0;
    
    // Step 15: Close nodes
    observerGraph.closeNode(&field);
    
    // Step 16: Return cleanup result
    return {notifiedBefore, notifiedAfter};
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/core/resource.h>
#include <reaction/graph/observer_graph.h>
#include <memory>
#include <string>
#include <type_traits>

namespace test_reaction {

struct VoidResourceSnapshot {
    bool retrievedIsVoid;
    bool usesSboStorage;
    bool nameAssigned;
    bool nameClearedAfterClose;
};

inline VoidResourceSnapshot test_resource_void_behavior(const std::string &nodeName) {
    // 1. Create shared Resource<Void> instance
    auto resource = std::make_shared<reaction::Resource<reaction::Void>>();
    
    // 2. Check if resource uses SBO storage
    bool uses_sbo = resource->uses_sbo();
    
    // 3. Get value from resource
    auto value = resource->get();
    
    // 4. Check if retrieved value is Void type
    bool is_void = std::is_same_v<decltype(value), reaction::Void>;
    
    // 5. Get observer graph instance
    auto &observer_graph = reaction::ObserverGraph::getInstance();
    
    // 6. Register resource node
    observer_graph.addNode(resource->getPtr());
    
    // 7. Assign node name
    resource->setName(nodeName);
    
    // 8. Check if name was assigned
    bool name_assigned = resource->getName() == nodeName;
    
    // 9. Close node
    resource->close();
    
    // 10. Check if name was cleared after close
    bool name_cleared = resource->getName().empty();
    
    // 11. Return void resource snapshot
    return VoidResourceSnapshot{
        .retrievedIsVoid = is_void,
        .usesSboStorage = uses_sbo,
        .nameAssigned = name_assigned,
        .nameClearedAfterClose = name_cleared
    };
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/core/observer_node.h>
#include <reaction/graph/observer_graph.h>
#include <memory>

namespace test_reaction {

class CountingObserver : public reaction::ObserverNode {
public:
    void valueChanged(bool changed = true) override {
        m_notifications++;
    }
    
    int notifications() const {
        return m_notifications;
    }

private:
    int m_notifications = 0;
};

struct ObserverNodeLinkSnapshot {
    int firstNotificationsAfterInitial;
    int firstNotificationsAfterUpdate;
    int secondNotificationsAfterUpdate;
    int thirdNotificationsAfterUpdate;
};

ObserverNodeLinkSnapshot test_observer_node_links() {
    auto counter = std::make_shared<CountingObserver>();
    auto source1 = std::make_shared<reaction::ObserverNode>();
    auto source2 = std::make_shared<reaction::ObserverNode>();
    auto source3 = std::make_shared<reaction::ObserverNode>();
    
    auto& graph = reaction::ObserverGraph::getInstance();
    graph.addNode(counter);
    graph.addNode(source1);
    graph.addNode(source2);
    graph.addNode(source3);
    
    source1->updateObservers({counter});
    
    source1->notify(true);
    
    int firstCount = counter->notifications();
    
    source1->updateObservers({});
    source2->updateObservers({counter});
    
    source1->notify(true);
    int firstAfterOld = counter->notifications();
    
    source2->notify(true);
    int secondAfterNew = counter->notifications();
    
    source3->notify(true);
    int thirdAfterThird = counter->notifications();
    
    counter->close();
    source1->close();
    source2->close();
    source3->close();
    
    return ObserverNodeLinkSnapshot{
        firstCount,
        firstAfterOld,
        secondAfterNew,
        thirdAfterThird
    };
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/reaction.h>
#include <cstdint>

namespace test_reaction {

struct AtomicCompoundSnapshot {
    int afterAddition;
    int afterMultiplication;
    int afterBitwiseAnd;
    int afterShift;
    int postIncrementReturn;
    int finalValue;
    int calcEvaluations;
};

/**
 * Function: Demonstrate atomic compound assignments on a reactive counter.
 *
 * @return AtomicCompoundSnapshot Snapshot with values after each compound operation.
 */
inline AtomicCompoundSnapshot test_atomic_compound_assign() {
    using namespace reaction;
    
    // 1. Create reactive variable
    auto reactive_var = var(0);
    
    // 2. Create counter to track calculation evaluations
    auto evaluation_counter = var(0);
    
    // 3. Create calculation to track value
    auto calc_value = calc([&]() {
        evaluation_counter += 1;
        return reactive_var.get();
    });
    
    // 4. Apply atomic addition
    atomicAddAssign(reactive_var, 5);
    int after_addition = calc_value();  // 5
    
    // 5. Get value after addition
    // 6. Apply atomic multiplication
    atomicMulAssign(reactive_var, 3);
    int after_multiplication = calc_value();  // 15
    
    // 7. Get value after multiplication
    // 8. Apply atomic bitwise AND
    atomicBitwiseAndAssign(reactive_var, 0xF);
    int after_bitwise_and = calc_value();  // 15
    
    // 9. Get value after bitwise AND
    // 10. Apply atomic left shift
    atomicLeftShiftAssign(reactive_var, 2);
    int after_shift = calc_value();  // 60
    
    // 11. Get value after shift
    // 12. Apply post-increment
    int post_increment_return = reactive_var.postIncrement();
    int final_value = reactive_var.get();  // 61
    
    // 13. Get final value
    // 14. Return atomic compound snapshot
    return AtomicCompoundSnapshot{
        after_addition,
        after_multiplication,
        after_bitwise_and,
        after_shift,
        post_increment_return,
        final_value,
        evaluation_counter.get()
    };
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

      We can update x and y to new values (say, a+1 and b+1) to trigger recomputation of the sum expression.

   For the boolean: if we start with true, then not_flag is false. Then we set flag to false, so not_flag becomes true.

output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/reaction.h>
#include <reaction/expression/expression_builders.h>

namespace test_reaction {

struct UnaryExpressionSnapshot {
    int initialNegatedSum;
    int updatedNegatedSum;
    bool logicalNotAfterFlip;
    int bitwiseNotValue;
};

/**
 * Function: Demonstrate unary expression builders and operators on reactive values.
 *
 * This function showcases how low-level expression builder helpers (`make_binary_op`,
 * `make_unary_op`) and high-level unary operators (`-`, `!`, `~`) cooperate with the
 * `expr` factory to produce reactive expressions.
 *
 * @param a First input value participating in the sum expression
 * @param b Second input value participating in the sum expression
 * @return UnaryExpressionSnapshot Snapshot of expression outputs before/after updates
 */
inline UnaryExpressionSnapshot test_unary_expression_builders(int a, int b) {
    // Create reactive variables
    auto var1 = reaction::var(a);
    auto var2 = reaction::var(b);
    auto boolVar = reaction::var(true);
    auto intVar = reaction::var(5);
    
    // Build negated sum expression
    auto sumExpr = var1 + var2;
    auto negSum = -sumExpr;
    
    // Build logical NOT expression
    auto notExpr = !boolVar;
    
    // Build bitwise NOT expression
    auto bitNotExpr = ~intVar;
    
    // Get initial values
    UnaryExpressionSnapshot snapshot;
    snapshot.initialNegatedSum = negSum.get();
    snapshot.logicalNotAfterFlip = notExpr.get();
    snapshot.bitwiseNotValue = bitNotExpr.get();
    
    // Update variables to trigger recomputation
    var1.value(a + 1);
    var2.value(b + 1);
    boolVar.value(false);
    intVar.value(10);
    
    // Get updated values
    snapshot.updatedNegatedSum = negSum.get();
    
    return snapshot;
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

 The function should:
  1. Create reactive boolean variables (using `reaction::var`)
  2. Build logical AND expression (using `&&` operator)
  3. Build logical OR expression (using `||` operator)
  4. Get initial AND and OR results (using `get` method)
  5. Update variables to trigger recomputation (using `value` method)
  6. Get updated AND and OR results
  7. Return a `LogicalExpressionSnapshot` struct with the results

 Steps in the function:
  - We'll create three reactive variables: a, b, c (using `reaction::var`).
  - Then we build two expressions: 
        auto and_expr = a && b;
        auto or_expr = a || b;
  - We then get the initial values of these expressions.
  - Then we update one or more variables (for example, set a to false, b to true, etc.) to trigger recomputation.
  - Then we get the updated values of the expressions.
  - Finally, we return a `LogicalExpressionSnapshot` with the initial and updated results.

 However, note that the problem says: "Update variables to trigger recomputation". We are allowed to update any of the variables (a, b, c) but note that the expressions only depend on a and b. So we can update a, b, or both.

 Let's choose to update a and b to see the effect. For example, we can set a to false and b to true.

 But note: the function parameters are the initial values of a, b, c. We are going to update the reactive variables, not the parameters.

 Implementation:

 We are to return a struct with:
   initialAnd: the result of (a && b) initially
   initialOr: the result of (a || b) initially
   updatedAnd: the result of (a && b) after update
   updatedOr: the result of (a || b) after update

output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/reaction.h>

namespace test_reaction {

struct LogicalExpressionSnapshot {
    bool initialAnd;
    bool initialOr;
    bool updatedAnd;
    bool updatedOr;
};

/**
 * Function: Demonstrate logical expression builders and operators on reactive values.
 *
 * This function shows how high-level logical operators (`&&`, `||`) compose reactive
 * expressions that are then lifted into `expr` calculations.
 *
 * @param a Initial value for the first boolean input
 * @param b Initial value for the second boolean input
 * @param c Initial value for the fallback boolean input
 * @return LogicalExpressionSnapshot Snapshot of AND/OR results before and after updates
 */
inline LogicalExpressionSnapshot test_logical_expression_builders(bool a, bool b, bool c) {
    // Create reactive boolean variables
    auto reactive_a = reaction::var(a);
    auto reactive_b = reaction::var(b);
    auto reactive_c = reaction::var(c);
    
    // Build logical expressions
    auto and_expr = reactive_a && reactive_b;
    auto or_expr = reactive_a || reactive_b;
    
    // Get initial results
    bool initial_and = and_expr.get();
    bool initial_or = or_expr.get();
    
    // Update variables to trigger recomputation
    reactive_a.value(!a);  // Flip a
    reactive_b.value(!b);  // Flip b
    
    // Get updated results
    bool updated_and = and_expr.get();
    bool updated_or = or_expr.get();
    
    return LogicalExpressionSnapshot{
        .initialAnd = initial_and,
        .initialOr = initial_or,
        .updatedAnd = updated_and,
        .updatedOr = updated_or
    };
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/cache/graph_cache.h>
#include <reaction/core/observer_node.h>
#include <memory>

namespace test_reaction {

struct NodeMetricsCacheSnapshot {
    std::size_t entriesBefore;
    std::size_t entriesAfterCache;
    std::size_t entriesAfterInvalidate;
    std::size_t hitCount;
    std::size_t missCount;
    double hitRatio;
    bool metricsCachedExists;
    bool metricsClearedAfterInvalidate;
};

NodeMetricsCacheSnapshot test_node_metrics_cache() {
    // 1. Create NodeMetricsCache instance
    auto cache = std::make_shared<reaction::NodeMetricsCache>();
    
    // 2. Create ObserverNode instance
    auto node = std::make_shared<reaction::ObserverNode>();
    
    // 3. Get cache statistics before caching
    auto stats_before = cache->getStats();
    
    // 4. Query metrics before caching
    auto metrics_before = cache->getNodeMetrics(node);
    
    // 5. Cache node metrics (explicitly)
    if (metrics_before) {
        cache->cacheNodeMetrics(node, *metrics_before);
    }
    
    // 6. Query metrics after caching
    auto metrics_after = cache->getNodeMetrics(node);
    
    // 7. Get cache statistics after caching
    auto stats_after_cache = cache->getStats();
    
    // 8. Invalidate all entries
    cache->invalidateAll();
    
    // 9. Query metrics after invalidation
    auto metrics_invalidated = cache->getNodeMetrics(node);
    
    // 10. Get cache statistics after invalidation
    auto stats_after_invalidate = cache->getStats();
    
    // 11. Return node metrics cache snapshot
    return NodeMetricsCacheSnapshot{
        .entriesBefore = stats_before.entries,
        .entriesAfterCache = stats_after_cache.entries,
        .entriesAfterInvalidate = stats_after_invalidate.entries,
        .hitCount = stats_after_cache.hitCount,
        .missCount = stats_after_cache.missCount,
        .hitRatio = stats_after_cache.hitRatio,
        .metricsCachedExists = (metrics_after != nullptr),
        .metricsClearedAfterInvalidate = (metrics_invalidated == nullptr)
    };
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/cache/cache_base.h>
#include <memory>
#include <chrono>

namespace test_reaction {

struct GenericCacheStatsSnapshot {
    std::size_t entriesBefore;
    std::size_t entriesAfterInsert;
    std::size_t entriesAfterInvalidate;
    std::size_t hitCount;
    std::size_t missCount;
    double hitRatio;
    bool firstLookupWasMiss;
    bool secondLookupWasHit;
};

class IntCache : public reaction::CacheBase<int, int> {
public:
    using reaction::CacheBase<int, int>::CacheBase;
    
    using reaction::CacheBase<int, int>::getStats;
    using reaction::CacheBase<int, int>::getCachedValue;
    using reaction::CacheBase<int, int>::cacheValue;
    using reaction::CacheBase<int, int>::invalidateAllEntries;
    using reaction::CacheBase<int, int>::triggerCleanup;
};

GenericCacheStatsSnapshot test_generic_cache_base() {
    IntCache cache;
    
    // 4. Get initial cache statistics
    auto initialStats = cache.getStats();
    GenericCacheStatsSnapshot snapshot;
    snapshot.entriesBefore = initialStats.totalEntries;
    
    // 5. Perform initial lookup (should be miss)
    const int key1 = 42;
    const int value1 = 100;
    auto firstResult = cache.getCachedValue(key1);
    snapshot.firstLookupWasMiss = (firstResult.has_value() == false);
    
    // 6. Insert value into cache
    cache.cacheValue(key1, value1);
    
    // 7. Perform second lookup (should be hit)
    auto secondResult = cache.getCachedValue(key1);
    snapshot.secondLookupWasHit = (secondResult.has_value() == true);
    
    // 8. Get cache statistics after insertion
    auto afterInsertStats = cache.getStats();
    snapshot.entriesAfterInsert = afterInsertStats.totalEntries;
    snapshot.hitCount = afterInsertStats.hitCount;
    snapshot.missCount = afterInsertStats.missCount;
    snapshot.hitRatio = afterInsertStats.hitRatio;
    
    // 9. Invalidate all cache entries
    cache.invalidateAllEntries();
    
    // 10. Trigger cleanup
    cache.triggerCleanup();
    
    // 11. Get cache statistics after invalidation
    auto afterInvalidStats = cache.getStats();
    snapshot.entriesAfterInvalidate = afterInvalidStats.totalEntries;
    
    return snapshot;
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

namespace test_reaction {

/**

 *
